title: Nigeria’s rate of extreme poverty
subtitle: Survey vs extrapolation estimates
source: World Bank Poverty and Inequality Platform
visdescription: Comparison of extrapolated versus actual survey-based poverty estimates for Nigeria (2018–22), showing how national accounts-based projections underestimated the true poverty rate revealed by the 2022 household survey.

scenes.text: In 2018, survey estimates revealed that around 34 percent of Nigerians (71 million people) were living in extreme poverty.
Without updated survey data, poverty monitoring between 2018 and 2022 relied on model-based projections, which estimated that the poverty rate had remained broadly stable, at 34.8 percent.
The 2022 survey data revealed that the poverty rate was actually 41.8 percent, substantially higher than the projected 34.8 percent.

The seven percentage point discrepancy corresponds roughly to [emphasis: an additional 17 million additional Nigerians living in extreme poverty], underscoring the limitations of modeling as a substitute for timely household survey data.

_meta.approvalNote: survey strip, Nigeria dual-line, waffles, SPI, SPI–GDP
